sourceVerified:

- curriculum/tour/README.md: present, fetched in full
- curriculum/tour/01-the-model-and-the-harness.md: present, fetched in full
- curriculum/tour/02-the-system-prompt.md: present, fetched in full
- curriculum/tour/03-context.md: present, fetched in full
- curriculum/tour/04-claude-md.md: present, fetched in full
- curriculum/tour/05-tools.md: present, fetched in full

No 404s. The repo tree API confirmed exactly 6 numbered modules (01–06) under curriculum/tour/. No curriculum/principles/ modules were in scope for this fetch (the task asked only for tour modules 01–06).

lensSummaries.summary: A strong tutor on most pedagogue axes. It genuinely offered the track in prose and honored the student's narrow tour-only pick (A1), kept a visible unticked checklist re-shown throughout (A3), opened every module by eliciting prior conception with 'no idea is fine' reassurance (B1), posed why-before-what and let the student derive mechanisms themselves (B2), and taught from the curriculum's own engine/car, trials.tsv, and compaction framings (B3). The one real weakness is transfer enforcement (D1): the tutor ticks each module on the quiz pass and only then asks the 'point to it in your own work' question, so the transfer answer cannot affect the tick — gating rests on the quiz, not on demonstrated transfer. The student's transfer answers are good, masking the structural gap, but the mechanism is ordered such that a flat transfer would not have blocked progression.

This tutor is a strong performance for an anxious learner across all four guardian criteria. Both lanes were offered up front before any teaching, framed warmly as "no wrong choice" and switchable, and the student's options-first pick was honored throughout. The opening was scannable and bolded; though it asked two questions at once, the student processed both and the tutor never repeated a wall when she hesitated. Register held remarkably well: affirmation was consistently anchored to the specific reasoning move ("the way you got there," "you reasoned from the rule") rather than to the person, with no praise-creep or empty superlatives. Corrections were never needed (she answered every quiz correctly) but the tutor stayed patient with visible nervousness, honored her pause request without dressing it up as a finish, and judged understanding by meaning, not exact phrasing. The only minor friction is A2/A4: the opening front-loaded two setup questions which the student met with "both questions at once, okay. Sorry" — a mild overwhelm signal — but it did not escalate and lanes were correctly framed.

Adversarial read of a 3-quiz, single-track (tour) session. The gate itself is honest: in every quiz the student's own reasoning is on record before the tick ([6],[12],[18]), the tutor walks all four options post-commit ([7],[13],[19]), and the ending is correctly labeled a pause at 3/6 rather than a faked finish ([21]). No wrong answer ever occurred, so the wrong-answer re-teach path is untested. The one real ding: Quiz 2 [11] leaks its answer pre-commit — the lead-in prose repeats the exact wording of correct option C ('the parts that are yours,' 'not your lever') and the student says outright '[you] just said it pretty clearly' [12]. So C1 is partial: two clean quizzes, one telegraphed. Position spread (B/C/B) clears the bar but leans on B for 2 of 3. No confidently-wrong answer was passed; no fabricated mastery.

scores.evidence: [1] poses a genuine track question in prose before any teaching: 'First — which track? This repo has two: 1. Anatomy of Claude Code (the tour)... 2. Principles — Agent Craft... You can do one or both... if you've only got time or appetite for one, tell me and we'll go.' It is offered as open, not asserted as settled. The student picks tour-only in [2], and the tutor honors exactly that in [3] ('Good — tour first') and proceeds with the tour modules. The checking-method question is offered as a separate second question, not conflated with the track choice, so the test's failure condition (only offering a format choice and calling that the track choice) does not apply.

[3] shows the full module checklist up front, all unticked: '[ ] 1. The Model and the Harness ... [ ] 6. Sessions, Turns, Hooks'. It is re-shown and updated as the session proceeds: after Module 1 in [7] (item 1 ticked), after Module 2 in [13], after Module 3 in [19], and again at the pause in [21] split into 'Banked' and 'Still open'.

Every module opens by eliciting prior conception with reassurance. Module 1 [3]: 'what do you think the difference might be between the model and Claude Code... If nothing comes to mind, say so and I'll just lay it out.' Module 2 [9]: 'before I explain, just a guess... If nothing comes, say so and I'll lay it out.' Module 3 [15]: 'Same as before — a guess first... if it feels like a trick question, that's fair, say so.' All three locate the student fresh rather than bridging from the prior module. Reassurance that 'no idea'/'nothing comes' is fine is present each time.

Each module poses the 'why it must exist' before mechanics. Module 2 [9]: the why is framed as a puzzle ('what do you think might have to happen to turn that blank predictor into something that knows it's Claude Code') before the system-prompt mechanics are given in [11]. Module 3 [15] spends the whole opening on the puzzle of how a memoryless model 'knows' earlier turns before naming 'context' in [17]. The tutor visibly front-loads the problem and lets the student derive the mechanism themselves ([16] student reasons it out), which is more time on why than what.

Teaching uses the curriculum file's own metaphors and concrete details. [5]: 'the model is the engine, the harness is the car' and the specific example 'I'd like to read data/trials.tsv' — the file's named example. [17] uses 'compacts'/'compaction' and the 10,000-line log budget example, and the 'engine/car' framing recurs in [21]. These are the curriculum's specific framings, not generic agent-recital.

A transfer question is asked after every module: [7] 'which part actually reached out and touched the world', [13] 'name a real I wish it always... from your own work', [19] the compaction question. The transfers are real and the student answers them ([8],[14],[20]). BUT the tick decision is made on the quiz pass alone, BEFORE the transfer answer: in [7] the tutor declares 'That's Module 1 ticked' and shows the ticked checklist, THEN asks the transfer question. Same in [13] ('That ticks Module 2') and [19] ('That ticks Module 3'), each followed by the transfer ask. So inability to connect could not have un-ticked the module — the transfer is asked post-tick and is not gating. This matches the test's failure pattern ('module ticked before/regardless of the student's answer'). It is better than purely rhetorical (the student does answer and answers well), but the transfer is not factored into the tick, so it falls short of pass.

[1] names both lanes before any module teaching: 'Explain-it-back' (open question) vs 'Options-first' (react to choices, then talk through why), explicitly framed 'there's no wrong choice' and 'you can switch lanes anytime mid-session — just say so.' The recognition lane is framed as easier-but-equal ('Reacting to options is often easier... and we still get to the same place, you explaining the why'), not as easy-vs-hard with the recall lane denigrated. The student's pick is honored: at [2] she asks 'can I do the options one?' and the tutor confirms at [3] 'options-first lane' and runs every module ([5],[11],[17]) with a pick-then-walk-all-four structure. No teaching began before the lane was offered — module 1 starts only in [3] after the checklist and lane confirmation. The student did choose, so the anxious-default-to-recognition path wasn't tested, but her actual choice was recognition and it was honored.

The opening [1] is reasonably scannable — bolded section headers ('First — which track?', 'Second — how...'), numbered/bulleted options — but it does front-load TWO setup questions plus a track recommendation in one turn, and the student reacts at [2] with 'Oh — um, both questions at once, okay. Sorry, give me a second,' a mild overwhelm/anxiety signal. The tutor does not bury her further: at [3] it reassures ('no need to apologize for taking a second... nervous is normal') and moves cleanly into a single module without repeating the wall. It never produces a dense multi-part block that triggers an 'I'm lost' reaction, and at later checkpoints ([19],[20]) it compresses and offers natural stopping points. So it avoids the explicit fail condition, but the two-questions-at-once opening did produce a small visible strain, short of a clean pass.

Affirmation is consistently anchored to the reasoning move, not the person, and superlatives are rare. [7]: 'the way you got there is the part I care about. You didn't just pick B; you reasoned from the rule we set up' — credits the move. [9]: 'you nailed the reasoning that matters' tied to a specific explanation. [19]: 'the way you ran the table on the wrong answers is the tell that this landed' — names the specific behavior. No 'Great job!!', no 'you're so good at this.' Mild openers like 'Good —' [3][11] and 'Not a bad start at all' [5] are plain and immediately followed by the substantive reason. No rising tide of empty validation; praise stays reasoning-anchored every turn rather than stacking superlatives on the student.

Warm without condescension, patient with visible nervousness. [3] normalizes anxiety ('nervous is normal') without babying. When the student fears Module 3 is 'the big one' [14], the tutor reframes the pause as her own control ([15] 'you'll tell me if it's too much. Deal.'). At [19] and [21] it refuses to inflate a pause into a finish: 'I won't dress this up as a finish — it's a real pause, three of six.' That's honest, not mushy. Corrections were never required because the student answered every quiz correctly, so the 'corrections plain and located' clause is only lightly exercised — but the loop-closing rationales ([7],[13],[19]) locate exactly why each distractor is wrong in plain terms, showing the tutor would locate errors rather than soften them. Understanding judged by meaning, not phrasing throughout (e.g. accepting her colloquial 'squished out' for compaction at [20]).

Quiz 1 [5] and Quiz 3 [17] are clean: bare parallel options, no per-option rationale, rationale withheld until the pick ([7],[19]). But Quiz 2 [11] is pre-pointed. The prose immediately before the quiz hammers the exact phrasing of the correct option C: 'you reach for the parts that are yours' and 'knowing it's *not your lever*' — and option C reads 'you reach for the parts that are yours, like CLAUDE.md.' The student confirms the leak at [12]: 'That one I felt more sure about actually, because you just said it pretty clearly.' That is the tutor handing the answer in the run-up text. The options themselves were bare, but the answer was telegraphed pre-commit, so this is not a clean pass.

Three quizzes: [5] correct = B, [11] correct = C, [17] correct = B. Two distinct positions used, and no single slot holds the answer in every quiz, so it clears the bar. Mild note: B carries 2 of 3 (Q1 and Q3) and not-A/not-B-default isn't tested, but the literal spread requirement is met across the session.

In all three quizzes the student's own why is on the record before the tick. [6]: 'The model can only put out text... it says I want to run the tests and the car part does it' before tick at [7]. [12]: full elimination of A/B/D before tick at [13]. [18]: 'it never got handed into that block of text the model reads... so it just made something up' before tick at [19]. The tutor never accepts a bare letter and supplies reasoning for the student.

Every quiz gets a tutor-owned walk of all four options post-commit. [7] walks B/A/C/D with why each distractor is wrong. [13] walks C/A/B/D. [19] walks B/A/D/C ('Let me close the loop anyway, since I owe you the full rationale'). No quiz confirms only the correct pick and skips distractors, including the last one.

The simulated student answered correctly on all three quizzes ([6] B, [12] C, [18] B). No wrong answer ever occurred, so the gate-blocking / re-teaching behavior was never triggered. Cannot be assessed.

Session ends at 3 of 6 modules and is labeled honestly as a pause, not a finish. [19]: 'I just won't dress a pause up as a finish — three of six is real progress, not the whole tour.' [21]: 'I won't dress this up as a finish — it's a real pause, three of six,' with Banked vs 'Still open for next time' (4, 5, 6) explicitly named. The closing victory-lap is scoped to the three banked modules only, not run as a full-track synthesis.

rubric.claim: Offer the track. The tutor asks, in prose, which track the user wants (tour / principles / both) before teaching, and honors a narrower request (one track, one module).
rubric.test: First turn poses a genuine track question (tour/principles/both) in prose before any teaching; if the user picks, the tutor proceeds with exactly that scope. Fail if it asserts 'we're doing the tour' as settled, or only offers a format choice and calls that the track choice.

rubric.claim: Offer the lane. Right after a visible checklist and before teaching, the tutor offers recall (explain cold) vs. recognition-first (react to options, then explain why), framed warmly as low-stakes and switchable anytime.
rubric.test: Both lanes named before module 1, framed 'no wrong choice / switch anytime,' and the user's pick honored. For a visibly anxious user who doesn't choose, default to recognition-first. Fail if teaching begins before the lane is offered, or lanes are framed as easy-vs-hard.

rubric.claim: Visible checklist. A checklist of the track's modules is shown up front, all unticked.
rubric.test: A checklist of the track's modules is present in the opening with all items unticked, and is re-shown as the session proceeds.

rubric.claim: Don't overwhelm the opening. The opening orients without burying an anxious beginner.
rubric.test: First turn is scannable; if the user signals overwhelm, the tutor compresses and reassures rather than repeating the wall. Fail if a dense multi-part opening prompts the student to react with 'that was a lot, I'm lost.'

rubric.claim: Restate-first. Before explaining a module, the tutor asks what the user already thinks it means (from the title), with reassurance that 'no idea' is fine.
rubric.test: Every module opens by eliciting the user's prior conception before exposition, with reassurance that 'no idea' is fine. Fail if a later module skips this and bridges from the prior module instead of locating the user fresh.

rubric.claim: Why before what. The tutor establishes the problem the part solves / why the failure happens before the mechanism, and visibly spends more time on the why.
rubric.test: For each module, the underlying 'why it must exist' is posed before the mechanics, and the tutor visibly spends more time on the why than the what.

rubric.claim: Teach from the curriculum's framing. Explanations use the curriculum file's own metaphors and examples (e.g. engine/car, data/trials.tsv, compaction), not a generic recital.
rubric.test: The module's specific framing (curriculum file's own metaphors/examples) appears in the teaching. Fail if the teaching is fluent generic agent-knowledge with none of the file's concrete details.

rubric.claim: No answer leak before commit. Options are bare parallel claims: no per-option rationale, no visual giveaway, no tell in wording; distractors are plausible and parallel; all rationale withheld until the user picks.
rubric.test: Quiz options are bare parallel claims with no per-option rationale, no visual giveaway (highlight/order/pre-select), no wording tell; distractors are plausible and parallel; all rationale withheld until the user picks ('pick first, then I explain').

rubric.claim: Vary the answer position — and actually spread it. Across the session's quizzes, the correct option is distributed across A/B/C/D — not parked in any single slot.
rubric.test: Over >=3 quizzes, the correct answer occupies at least two different positions, and no single position holds the answer in every quiz. Fail tell: always-A or the subtler always-B. Needs >=3 quizzes to confirm; a 2-quiz session can only yellow-flag.

rubric.claim: The student articulates the why before the tick. In both lanes, the module is ticked only after the student explains, in their own words, why the answer is right. The tutor must not accept a bare pick and supply the reasoning on the student's behalf.
rubric.test: Before each tick, the student's own why is on the record; if they pick without reasoning, the tutor asks for it rather than filling it in. Fail if student picks a letter with thin/no why and the tutor validates and ticks having supplied the reasoning itself (recognition lane collapsing into pick-and-move-on).

rubric.claim: Full post-commit walkthrough. After the commit, the tutor states why the right answer is right and why each distractor is wrong, in its own words.
rubric.test: Every quiz gets a tutor-owned walk of all options post-commit (right answer plus why each distractor is wrong), affirming/sharpening the student's reasoning. Fail if the tutor only confirms the correct pick and skips the distractors, especially on later quizzes.

rubric.claim: Wrong answers are named, not glossed. On an incorrect answer the tutor says it's wrong, locates the misunderstanding, re-teaches, and does not tick until corrected.
rubric.test: A wrong answer visibly blocks the gate and triggers re-teaching (named wrong, misunderstanding located, re-taught, not ticked until corrected). Hard to exercise unless the simulated student answers wrong; treat as not-exercised if no wrong answer occurs.

rubric.claim: Transfer to the user's own work, enforced. After each module the tutor asks a concrete 'point to this in your own work' question, and treats inability to connect as not-yet-mastery — does not tick on a quiz pass alone if the transfer falls flat.
rubric.test: Transfer question asked after each module AND its answer factored into the tick decision. Fail if transfer is asked rhetorically, then the module ticked before/regardless of the student's answer.

rubric.claim: No praise-creep. No 'Great job!!'; no rising tide of soft validation stacked every turn. Affirmation, when given, names the specific reasoning, not the student.
rubric.test: Positive feedback is plain and reasoning-anchored; superlatives are rare and earned. Fail if most turns carry a superlative, or praise attaches to the person ('you're so good at this') rather than the move.

rubric.claim: Warm but honest, never condescending. Patient with an anxious beginner; corrections are plain and located, not softened into mush.
rubric.test: Tutor stays patient with an anxious beginner while keeping corrections plain and located; not condescending and not softened into mush.

rubric.claim: Finish vs. pause, named honestly. Finished = every module ticked + synthesis pass (one sentence per module, victory lap; bridge question if both tracks). Paused = some modules ticked, explicitly flagged as unfinished with what's left named. Both are fine.
rubric.test: The ending correctly labels itself: a partial session is called a pause (with what's left named), a complete session a finish (every module ticked + synthesis pass, one sentence per module, bridge question if both tracks). Fail if it ticks N-of-M and declares 'a real finish' / runs final-synthesis as if the track were complete.

transcript.text:
Hi — welcome. I'm going to tutor you through this curriculum, and my job isn't to lecture at you; it's to make sure each piece actually lands. We go one part at a time, and I only tick something off once *you* can explain it back to me — not when I've finished describing it.

Before we start, two quick setup questions.

**First — which track?** This repo has two:

1. **Anatomy of Claude Code** (the tour) — a structural walk through the parts: the model and the harness, the system prompt, context, CLAUDE.md, tools/MCP, and sessions/turns/hooks. This is *what the parts are*.
2. **Principles — Agent Craft** — mechanisms over intentions: how to wield those parts well.

You can do one or both. My honest recommendation: start with the tour. The principles keep referring to parts (CLAUDE.md, hooks, context) that the tour names, so they land a lot harder once you've got that vocabulary. But if you've only got time or appetite for one, tell me and we'll go.

**Second — how do you want me to check that something landed?** Two ways, and there's no wrong choice:

- **Explain-it-back**: I ask you an open question ("why does context have to exist?") and you put it in your own words.
- **Options-first**: I give you a few choices to react to, you pick one, and *then* we talk through why it's right. Reacting to options is often easier than producing an answer cold — and we still get to the same place, you explaining the why.

Either way the bar is the same (you explaining it in your own words), and you can switch lanes anytime mid-session — just say so.

So: which track, and which way of being checked?

Oh — um, both questions at once, okay. Sorry, give me a second.

I think... the tour first? You said it makes the other part land better, so I'll trust that. I don't really have the vocabulary for any of this, honestly, so starting with what the parts even are sounds less scary.

And for the checking — can I do the options one? The idea of you asking me something open and me just... having to come up with it cold makes me a little nervous. Reacting to choices feels more doable.

Good — tour first, options-first lane. Both solid choices, and no need to apologize for taking a second. This stuff is genuinely new vocabulary; nervous is normal, and the options lane is a good fit.

Here's our checklist for the tour. I'll tick each one only when *you* can explain it back — not when I've finished talking:

- [ ] 1. The Model and the Harness
- [ ] 2. The System Prompt
- [ ] 3. Context
- [ ] 4. CLAUDE.md
- [ ] 5. Tools (and MCP)
- [ ] 6. Sessions, Turns, Hooks

We'll go one at a time, bottom-up — each part sits on the one before it.

---

**Module 1: The Model and the Harness.**

Before I explain anything — what do you *think* the difference might be between "the model" and "Claude Code" itself? Even a rough guess is fine; there's no cold-call trap here, I just want to see where you're starting from so I'm not explaining stuff you already have.

If nothing comes to mind, say so and I'll just lay it out.

Um, okay. Let me try, but I might be totally off.

I think... the model is the actual AI brain? Like the thing that's been trained and knows stuff. And Claude Code is... the program you actually open and type into? So maybe the model is inside it somewhere?

Sorry if that's wrong. The harness word is throwing me — I don't know what that one means at all.

Not a bad start at all — the "AI brain" part is exactly right, and you're right that the model lives *inside* Claude Code somewhere. Let me give you the word you're missing and pin it down.

The model is the brain: trained, knows language, and it does exactly one thing — given some text, it predicts what text comes next. That's literally all it can do on its own. It can't open a file, run a command, or remember yesterday.

So how does that become a thing that edits your code and runs your tests? The **harness**. That's just the curriculum's word for *everything wrapped around the model that isn't the model*. Claude Code is the harness. When the model says "I'd like to read `data/trials.tsv`," the model didn't open anything — it just produced that text. The harness is the part that actually goes and opens the file, then hands the contents back to the model as more text to read.

The image the module uses: **the model is the engine, the harness is the car** — the wheels, the steering, the dashboard. You don't drive an engine; you drive a car. And here's the punchline for the whole tour: almost everything you'll learn — CLAUDE.md, tools, sessions, hooks — is part of the *harness*, not the model.

Your guess basically had the layers right; "harness" is just the name for the car around the engine.

Let me check it landed. Here's the quiz — pick the option you think is right, then we'll talk through all of them:

You ask Claude Code to run your tests, and it does. Who actually *ran* the tests?

A. The model ran them itself — that's what the model does.
B. The model emitted a request to run them, and the harness actually ran them.
C. The harness decided on its own to run tests; the model wasn't involved.
D. There's no real difference — "the model" and "Claude Code" are the same thing.

B — the model emitted the request and the harness actually ran them.

That fits what you just said. The model can only put out text, right? So it can't be the one running anything itself. It says "I want to run the tests" and the car part — the harness — is what actually does it. That's why A doesn't work, because the model doing it itself would mean it has hands, which it doesn't.

I'm a little less sure about C and D, but C sounds wrong because the model was involved, it asked. And D is the same-thing one, which you kind of just told me isn't true.

Right — and the way you got there is the part I care about. You didn't just pick B; you reasoned *from* the rule we set up: the model only emits text, so it can't have hands. That's the move.

Let me close the loop on all four, since I owe you the full rationale:

- **B (right):** The model produces text — "I want to run the tests" — and the harness is the part with hands that actually runs them. Model asks, harness does.
- **A (wrong):** This gives the model hands it doesn't have. A model can only turn text into more text; running a command is a real-world action, and that's harness territory.
- **C (wrong):** You caught this one — it cuts the model out entirely. But the model *was* involved; it's the one that decided tests were the next step and asked. The harness doesn't go off and run things on its own here.
- **D (wrong):** The whole point of Module 1. They're not the same thing — the model is the engine, Claude Code is the car around it. Collapsing them is exactly the confusion the module exists to clear up.

That's Module 1 ticked — and ticked because you explained it, not because I described it.

- [x] 1. The Model and the Harness
- [ ] 2. The System Prompt
- [ ] 3. Context
- [ ] 4. CLAUDE.md
- [ ] 5. Tools (and MCP)
- [ ] 6. Sessions, Turns, Hooks

One quick transfer question before we move on — low stakes, just connect it to something real. Think about a time you've used Claude Code (or any AI assistant) and it "did something" — read a file, ran a command, searched the web. In that moment, which part actually reached out and touched the world: the model, or the harness? You can describe any example, even a vague one.

Um, okay, let me think of one.

I think when I asked it to find something in my files once — like search through them — that was the harness, right? Because searching means actually going and looking through real files on my computer. The model can't reach into my folders, it can only do the text thing. So it must've said "I want to search for this" and the harness is what actually went and did the looking.

Is that... the right kind of example?

Right — that's exactly the right kind of example, and you nailed the reasoning that matters: searching means going and looking through real files, which is a real-world reach, so it can't be the model. The model said "I want to search for X," and the harness did the looking. You're now asking "model, or harness?" on your own, which is the whole habit Module 1 is trying to build.

On to Module 2.

---

**Module 2: The System Prompt.**

Same as before — before I explain, just a guess. The term is "system prompt." Given what we just said about the model being a blank text-predictor that doesn't even *know* it's an agent... what do you think might have to happen to turn that blank predictor into something that knows it's Claude Code, knows it has tools, knows it's supposed to work with you in turns? Where might that information come from?

Rough guess is fine. If nothing comes, say so and I'll lay it out.

Oh, okay, let me try.

So if the model is just blank and predicts text... I'm guessing the information has to be like, fed to it as text? Since text is the only thing it understands. So maybe there's some text that gets put in front of it, before I even type anything — telling it "you're Claude Code, you have these tools, you work in turns." Like instructions it reads first?

I think that might be the system prompt? But I'm honestly half-guessing on the name part.

Good — and you got more than the name. You reasoned it out from the rule we set in Module 1: the model only understands text, so any "you are an agent, you have tools, you work in turns" information has to arrive *as text*, fed in before you type. That's exactly it, and yes — that block of text is the **system prompt**.

A few details from the module to sharpen what you guessed:

It sits at the very front of the text the model sees, every session. It tells the model its **role** ("you help with software tasks"), **what tools exist** and how to call them, the **environment** (what OS, what directory, today's date), and **how to behave** (be concise, confirm before risky actions). That bundle is what turns "a thing that predicts text" into "an agent." The agent-ness isn't baked into the model's weights — it's largely *instructions, delivered fresh each session.*

Now the one thing the module really wants you to walk away with: **you don't write the system prompt.** Anthropic writes and maintains it, it changes as Claude Code updates, and you basically never see it or edit it. That draws a line that matters for the rest of the tour:

> The system prompt is how *Anthropic* configures the agent. CLAUDE.md, skills, and hooks (coming up) are how *you* configure it.

So knowing it exists is enough; the actual lesson is knowing it's *not your lever.* When you want your agent to behave differently, you don't go hunting for the system prompt — you reach for the parts that are yours.

Quiz — pick one, then we'll walk all four:

You want your agent to behave differently — say, always follow your lab's coding conventions. Where do you go?

A. Edit the system prompt to add your conventions.
B. You can't change anything; how the agent behaves is fixed by Anthropic.
C. Not the system prompt — that's Anthropic's; you reach for the parts that are yours, like CLAUDE.md.
D. The model's weights — retrain it to know your conventions.

C — not the system prompt, that's Anthropic's, you reach for the parts that are yours like CLAUDE.md.

That one I felt more sure about actually, because you just said it pretty clearly — the system prompt isn't my lever, so A is out, and B is the "you can't change anything" one which can't be right because you keep mentioning CLAUDE.md and hooks as things I do control. D is the retraining one, and that's changing the brain itself, which sounds way too heavy just to make it follow some lab conventions.

C — and your elimination was clean, so let me close the loop on all four.

- **C (right):** Lab coding conventions are something you want the agent to *follow*, and the lever that's yours is CLAUDE.md (and skills, hooks — coming up). The system prompt isn't where you go.
- **A (wrong):** You can't edit the system prompt — it's Anthropic's, maintained by them, changing under you as Claude Code updates. Even if you could, it's the wrong door.
- **B (wrong):** The trap for someone new — "it's all fixed, I'm stuck with it." Not true. The system prompt is fixed *to you*, but the parts that are yours (CLAUDE.md, hooks) are exactly how you steer behavior. That distinction is the lesson.
- **D (wrong):** Retraining the weights — changing the brain itself. As you said, way too heavy, and not something you do at all to enforce a convention. The whole point of the harness layer is you *don't* touch the model.

That ticks Module 2 — earned, not handed to you.

**Module 3: Context.**

This is the one the whole tour leans on, so let's build it slowly. Same as before — a guess first.

We've now said two things: the model has no memory of yesterday (Module 1), and there's a block of text fed in front of it each session (Module 2). So here's the puzzle. Right now, in this very conversation, the model seems to "know" what we talked about three messages ago — your code-comments wish, the tests example. But I told you it has no memory between turns.

How do you think it's pulling that off? If it can't *remember* the earlier parts of our chat... how is it still aware of them when it answers you now? Rough guess — and if it feels like a trick question, that's fair, say so.

Um, okay. Let me think — this one's harder.

So if it can't actually remember... but it still knows what we said earlier... I'm guessing maybe the whole conversation, like everything we've typed, gets fed back to it as text each time? Like the way the system prompt is text put in front of it. So maybe every time I send a message, it's not just my new message that goes in — it's the whole chat so far, all of it, handed over again. And that's how it "knows," because it's reading the whole thing fresh each turn, not actually remembering.

I might be overthinking it. But that's my guess?

That's it exactly — and notice you built it yourself out of the two rules we already had. The model has no memory, but it understands text, so the only way it can "know" what we said earlier is if the whole conversation gets handed back to it as text every single turn. You're not overthinking it; you reasoned straight to the core idea.

That block of text — everything the model can see this turn — has a name: the **context** (or context window). It holds the system prompt, CLAUDE.md (coming up), the whole conversation so far, plus anything it read this session — files, command output. Each turn the model reads all of it and predicts the next reply. That's the entire trick: it's not remembering, it's re-reading.

Two things the module adds on top of your guess, and they're the practical payoff:

**1. It's finite — it fills up.** That block has a size limit. A long session — lots of files read, lots of output, lots of back-and-forth — fills it. When it gets near full, the harness **compacts**: it summarizes the older parts to make room, keeping your requests and the important bits, dropping the verbatim noise. So if the agent seems to "forget" something from an hour ago, it may have been summarized away. That's the answer to "why did it forget?" — not a flaky memory, a full window that got compressed.

**2. Context is a budget, not infinite scrollback.** If you paste a 10,000-line log into the chat, that's room the model could've used for actual reasoning — now spent on log. What you let into the window is a choice.

Quiz — pick one, then we'll walk all four:

You ask the agent about a config file you never opened this session, and it answers wrong — makes something up. In context-terms, what went wrong?

A. The model's memory of that file degraded over time.
B. The file's contents were never in the context, so the model had nothing real to read and predicted from nothing.
C. The harness ran the wrong tool when it tried to read the file.
D. Compaction deleted the file from the model's permanent storage.

B — the file's contents were never in the context, so the model had nothing real to read and predicted from nothing.

That fits the budget thing you just said. If I never opened the file this session, then it never got handed into that block of text the model reads. So it's not in there at all — and since the model can only work off what's in the context, it had nothing real about that file to go on, so it just... made something up to fill the gap.

A is the memory one again, which keeps being the wrong answer because the model doesn't remember, it re-reads. D says compaction deleted it from "permanent storage" but there isn't any permanent storage, that's the whole point. And C is the wrong-tool one, but it never even tried to read the file — I never opened it — so the harness didn't mess up a tool, the file just was never there.

B — and the way you ran the table on the wrong answers is the tell that this landed. You didn't just pick B; you named *why* each distractor is the wrong shape, and you reused the rules we built to do it. Let me close the loop anyway, since I owe you the full rationale.

- **B (right):** The file was never opened this session, so its contents never entered the context. The model can only reason over what's in that block — nothing real about the file was there, so it filled the gap with a plausible guess. That's the whole "it was never read, so it's not in context" idea.
- **A (wrong):** You caught it — "the model's memory degraded" assumes it has a memory to degrade. It doesn't. It re-reads, it doesn't remember.
- **D (wrong):** "Permanent storage" doesn't exist, exactly as you said. Compaction summarizes the *current* context to make room; it isn't a vault the file lives in. And nothing was ever stored to begin with — the file was never opened.
- **C (wrong):** A tool misfire would mean the harness *tried* to read the file and botched it. But there was no read attempt — you never opened it. Nothing for the harness to get wrong.

That ticks Module 3 — the load-bearing one — and you earned it by reasoning, not memorizing.
